markdown.markdown:
TypeScript 4.1 introduced template literal types, bringing string manipulation to the type level. They're more useful than you might think.
## Basic Syntax
Template literal types work like JavaScript template literals, but for types:
```typescript
type Greeting = `hello ${string}`;

const a: Greeting = "hello world";  // OK
const b: Greeting = "hello";        // OK
const c: Greeting = "goodbye world"; // Error
```
## Typed Route Strings
```typescript
type ApiRoute = `/api/${"users" | "posts" | "comments"}`;
// "/api/users" | "/api/posts" | "/api/comments"

type DetailRoute = `${ApiRoute}/${number}`;
// "/api/users/${number}" | "/api/posts/${number}" | ...
```
## Event Name Patterns
```typescript
type EventTarget = "user" | "post" | "comment";
type EventAction = "created" | "updated" | "deleted";
type EventName = `${EventTarget}:${EventAction}`;
// "user:created" | "user:updated" | "user:deleted" | "post:created" | ...

function onEvent(name: EventName, handler: () => void) { }

onEvent("user:created", () => { });  // OK
onEvent("user:renamed", () => { });  // Error
```
## CSS Units
```typescript
type CSSUnit = "px" | "rem" | "em" | "%" | "vh" | "vw";
type CSSValue = `${number}${CSSUnit}`;

function setWidth(value: CSSValue) { }

setWidth("100px");   // OK
setWidth("1.5rem");  // OK
setWidth("100");     // Error: missing unit
setWidth("wide");    // Error
```
## Built-in String Manipulation
TypeScript provides utility types for transforming string types:
```typescript
type Upper = Uppercase<"hello">;      // "HELLO"
type Lower = Lowercase<"HELLO">;      // "hello"
type Cap = Capitalize<"hello">;       // "Hello"
type Uncap = Uncapitalize<"Hello">;   // "hello"
```
Combine them for real patterns:
```typescript
type PropGetter<T extends string> = `get${Capitalize<T>}`;
type PropSetter<T extends string> = `set${Capitalize<T>}`;

type NameGetter = PropGetter<"name">; // "getName"
type NameSetter = PropSetter<"name">; // "setName"
```
---
Template literal types turn strings from opaque blobs into structured, type-checked values. They're especially powerful for APIs where string patterns matter — routes, events, CSS values, and configuration keys.
